content: Blockchain records transactions through a decentralized ledger maintained collectively by a distributed network of nodes, eliminating the need for a central bank or intermediary. Each transaction is broadcast to the network and grouped into blocks. These blocks are cryptographically linked via hash pointers, creating an immutable chain. Consensus mechanisms—such as Proof of Work or Proof of Stake—ensure agreement on the transaction history across all nodes, preventing double-spending and fraud without centralized authority. Every participant holds a copy of the ledger, enabling transparent verification and resilience against single points of failure. The cryptographic structure guarantees data integrity, while economic incentives align validators’ behavior with network security. This architecture not only obviates trust in a central institution but also facilitates censorship resistance and global accessibility, redefining transaction settlement through distributed consensus rather than centralized control.
